title: Statistical Mechanics of Household Income and Wealth: Derivation from Firm Dynamics via Maximum Entropy and Mixture Aggregation
abstract: The distribution of income and wealth in developed economies exhibits a robust two-class structure: an exponential (Boltzmann--Gibbs) bulk covering $\sim\!97\%$ of the population, and a power-law (Pareto) tail in the upper $\sim\!3\%$. We derive this structure from first principles via an explicit mechanistic chain: Gibrat's law for firm growth implies a Zipf firm-size distribution; maximum entropy applied to within-firm wages, combined with mixture aggregation across firms, yields a Boltzmann--Gibbs income distribution with temperature $T_y$ for employees; additive-noise wealth dynamics with a reflecting wall at zero produce a Boltzmann--Gibbs employee wealth distribution with temperature $T_w$. For firm owners, multiplicative capital returns produce a Pareto wealth tail with exponent $α_w = 1/θ$, where $θ$ encodes how total returns scale with firm size. The empirical value $α_w \approx 1.30$ \cite{Yakovenko2009} is reproduced with no tuned parameters from the observed firm value scaling $V = V_0(s/s_0)^{0.77}$~\cite{Axtell2001}, and simultaneously yields the first quantitative estimate of the returns-per-employee size exponent: $ζ= θ- 1 \approx -0.23$. For empirical values $ν\approx 0.3$, $c \approx 0.81$, $k \approx 0.15$ (BEA long-run savings rate $\approx 5\%$), the model gives $T_w/T_y \approx 1.7\,\text{yr}$, i.e.\ lower-class households hold roughly 1--2 years of income as wealth, with the precise ratio depending on savings and tax rates and testable cross-country. As a parameter-free empirical test, firms near zero profit have a cash martingale whose first-passage time gives establishment exit rate $\sim t^{-1/2}$; convolving with the Zipf firm-size distribution yields firm-level exit rate $\sim t^{-1/2}\!\log t$, with apparent exponent $b = 0.295 \pm 0.03$, confirmed against BDS firm-age data with no free parameters.
summary: This paper provides a statistical mechanics derivation of the observed two-class structure in economic inequality. It connects firm-level dynamics, specifically Gibrat's law and firm-size scaling, to the emergence of a Boltzmann-Gibbs distribution for the majority and a Pareto tail for the wealthy. The model successfully reproduces empirical wealth exponents using observed firm-value scaling without any free parameters. It also predicts the ratio of wealth to income for lower-class households and firm exit rates.
main_problem: The lack of a mechanistic link between firm-level microeconomics and the observed two-class structure of household income and wealth (exponential Boltzmann-Gibbs bulk vs. power-law Pareto tail).
main_result: The paper derives the two-class income/wealth structure from first principles, showing that the Pareto exponent for wealth is directly determined by firm-size scaling without tuned parameters.
method: The study uses Fokker-Planck equations, Maximum Entropy principles, and mixture aggregation, validated by Monte Carlo agent simulations and empirical data from Compustat and BDS.
model_or_system: A three-sector economy consisting of Firms, Households (Employees and Owners), and Government, modeled via Gibrat's Law for firm growth and additive/multiplicative noise dynamics for wealth.
key_observables: Pareto wealth exponent (alpha_w), firm-size exponent (theta), wealth-to-income temperature ratio (Tw/Ty), and firm exit rates.
important_parameters: Firm-value scaling (theta), returns-per-employee size exponent (zeta), and the crossover point (x1) determined by demography.
main_assumptions: Within-firm wages follow Maximum Entropy distributions; firm growth follows Gibrat's Law (multiplicative Itô noise); wealth dynamics for employees involve a reflecting boundary at zero.
figures_summary: Figure 1(a) provides a money flow diagram illustrating the interactions and fiscal flows between the firm, household, and government sectors.
paper_structure: The paper establishes a mechanistic chain from firm growth (Gibrat's Law) to firm size (Zipf), then applies Maximum Entropy and mixture aggregation to derive income/wealth distributions, followed by empirical validation using firm-value and exit-rate data.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) Three-sector money flow diagram. Solid arrows: flows determining the distributions (wages, consumption, capital returns). Dashed arrows: fiscal flows (taxes, government spending), shown to leave the GB form robust. Employees (⊕additive wages) yield BG income and wealth; Owners (⊗multiplicative returns) yield Pareto income and wealth. (b) Survival functions S(x) = P(X > x) on log–log axes, from Monte Carlo simulation (dots) and analytic fits (dashed lines), population-weighted (femp = 0.97, fown = 0.03).
